title: Should Jersey Ditch the Royals for Its Next Banknote Redesign?
description: As English banknotes transition to King Charles III, Jersey faces a decision on whether to refresh its 2010 currency designs and follow Guernsey's lead in spotlighting local culture over royal portraits.
lede: English banknotes bearing King Charles III entered circulation in 2024, but Jersey's currency is still stuck in 2010 with the late Queen Elizabeth II. With Guernsey already choosing to ditch royal portraits for its next 2027 series, pressure is mounting on Jersey to decide what best represents its unique culture and economy.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Bank of England notes featuring King Charles III officially entered circulation in 2024. Meanwhile, Jersey's own banknotes were last refreshed in 2010 and still prominently feature the late Queen Elizabeth II alongside local landmarks.

Guernsey has already taken a decisive step. In 2024, the neighboring island announced that its next series of notes, expected to roll out from 2027, will not feature a portrait of King Charles. Instead, Guernsey is opening the door for local artists to propose fresh, culturally relevant designs.

Jersey's Treasury and Exchequer explored the possibility of a redesign in 2025. However, the government confirmed that it still holds 'a significant stock' of the current D-class family of notes, delaying any immediate changes.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: Banknotes offer a revealing snapshot of how a jurisdiction views itself. For an island where finance and farming heavily coexist, the currency acts as a global calling card for its culture and economic priorities.

Past series have highlighted the island's unique ecology, such as the 1989 notes showcasing Dartford warblers and brent geese, while current designs include a Jersey cow watermark. A redesign is an opportunity to rebrand and modernise the island's image for the decades ahead.

sections.heading: The Economics of Redesign
paragraphs: Government figures show that approximately £93 million worth of Jersey notes were in circulation at the end of 2025. Managing this supply is a delicate balancing act for the local Treasury.

Minimum print runs are required to achieve value for money in a smaller jurisdiction like Jersey. Exhausting the current stockpiles before commissioning new designs is a calculated move to avoid unnecessary costs and maintain value on behalf of the public.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: While a redesign is not imminent, the Treasury has stated that a public consultation is one option under consideration for the next series of Jersey notes. This would give residents a direct say in what deserves pride of place on their money.

Until the current reserves are depleted, it is likely to be some time before any final decisions are made. However, the debate over whether to follow Guernsey's lead or stick to tradition is now firmly on the table.
